front: Motivation theories — how do Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor, McClelland, Vroom and Pink compare?

legend.meaning: Needs in five ascending levels; lower needs must be largely met before higher ones motivate
Two factors — hygiene (pay, conditions) prevents dissatisfaction; motivators (achievement, growth) drive satisfaction
Theory X (people avoid work, need control) vs Theory Y (self-motivated, seek responsibility) — the manager's assumptions
Three acquired needs — achievement, affiliation, power; match the work to a person's dominant need
Expectancy — motivation = expectancy x instrumentality x valence; people weigh the odds of effort leading to a valued reward
Intrinsic drive for knowledge work — autonomy, mastery, purpose (carrots and sticks backfire)

back:
All explain what energises people, and most separate true motivators from baseline conditions that merely prevent unhappiness.

They differ in lens: Maslow ranks needs; Herzberg splits factors into two types; McGregor is about the manager's beliefs; McClelland matches the individual's dominant need; Vroom is a calculation of perceived odds; Pink rejects rewards/punishments for creative work in favour of intrinsic drivers.
tip: Herzberg trap: a pay rise or better conditions are hygiene — they remove dissatisfaction but do not motivate. Recognition, growth and purpose do.
source: Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor, McClelland, Vroom, Pink — motivation theories

front: Team models — Tuckman vs Drexler-Sibbet vs Belbin?

legend.meaning: Five stages a team passes through — forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning
Seven stages with a key question to resolve at each, from creating the team to sustaining and renewing it
Nine behavioural ROLES people adopt (action, people, thinking) — about team composition, not stages

back:
Tuckman and Drexler-Sibbet both describe how a team MATURES over time, and both say high performance comes only after early friction.

The difference: Tuckman is the simple five-stage view; Drexler-Sibbet is a richer seven-stage view with a question to answer at each. Belbin is orthogonal — it describes the MIX of roles in the team, not a sequence.
tip: Stages (Tuckman, Drexler-Sibbet) say WHEN a team performs; Belbin roles say WHO does what. Don't conflate a stage model with a role model.
source: Tuckman; Drexler-Sibbet; Belbin — team development and roles

front: Conflict models — Thomas-Kilmann modes vs Pondy's stages?

legend.meaning: Five ways to HANDLE conflict — competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating (by assertiveness x cooperativeness)
Five STAGES a conflict moves through — latent, perceived, felt, manifest, aftermath

back:
Both are five-part models of workplace conflict, but they answer different questions.

Thomas-Kilmann is about the RESPONSE you choose in the moment; Pondy is about the LIFECYCLE a conflict goes through over time. PMI favours collaborating (confront/problem-solve) for the most durable resolution, with the right mode depending on stakes and time.
tip: TKI = how to respond (modes); Pondy = how a conflict develops (stages). Collaborate is the PMI-preferred mode.
source: Thomas-Kilmann; Pondy — conflict mode and conflict stages

front: Leadership styles — Situational vs Servant vs Transformational/Transactional?

legend.meaning: Adapt your style — directing, coaching, supporting, delegating — to the follower's competence and commitment
Lead by serving the team first — remove impediments, grow people; central to agile
Inspire and elevate around a vision vs exchange rewards for performance

back:
All are relationship and behaviour styles, and none is universally best.

Situational leadership flexes to the person and task; servant leadership puts the team's needs first; transformational inspires change while transactional trades rewards for results. The PMP mindset and agile lean servant and situational.
tip: There is no single best style. Agile favours servant leadership; Situational says match the style to the follower's readiness.

front: Change models — Lewin vs Kotter vs ADKAR vs Bridges vs Satir?

legend.meaning: Three steps — unfreeze, change, refreeze
Eight steps, org-wide — from creating urgency to anchoring change in the culture
Individual adoption — Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement
The psychological TRANSITION — Ending, Neutral Zone, New Beginning (separate from the external change)
The performance curve — late status quo, resistance, chaos (a dip), integration, new status quo

back:
All address the PEOPLE side of change, not the technical rollout.

Lewin is the simplest three-phase frame; Kotter is an organisation-level process; ADKAR works one individual at a time; Bridges focuses on the inner psychological transition versus the external event; Satir normalises the temporary drop in performance that change causes.
tip: Bridges and Satir describe the human transition (slower); Lewin and Kotter describe the change process; ADKAR works person by person.
source: Lewin; Kotter; Prosci ADKAR; Bridges; Satir — change models

legend.meaning: Five domains — clear, complicated, complex, chaotic, disorder — each with its own response, from best practice to emergent
A grid of agreement (on requirements) against certainty (on approach), pointing to simple, complicated, complex or chaotic

back:
Both sort a situation by its uncertainty so you can choose predictive versus adaptive delivery, and both say more uncertainty or less agreement calls for experimentation.

Cynefin names five domains with response heuristics; Stacey plots two continuous dimensions — how much people agree and how certain the approach is.
tip: Both say: more uncertainty or disagreement points to adaptive delivery. Cynefin names domains; Stacey plots agreement x certainty.

front: Quality thinkers — Deming vs Juran vs Crosby vs Ishikawa?

legend.meaning: Systems thinking, PDCA and 14 points; most problems come from the system, not the worker
Quality Trilogy — planning, control, improvement; 'fitness for use'; popularised the Pareto principle
'Quality is free'; Zero Defects; quality is conformance to requirements, achieved by prevention
Cause-and-effect (fishbone) diagram, quality circles, and the seven basic quality tools

back:
All four champion prevention over inspection — quality is planned and built in, then measured and improved continuously.

Their emphasis differs: Deming on the system and PDCA; Juran on fitness for use and the trilogy; Crosby on zero defects and the cost of quality; Ishikawa on practical tools like the fishbone.
tip: Prevention over inspection is the shared creed. 'Quality is free' = Crosby; 'fitness for use' = Juran; PDCA and the system = Deming; fishbone = Ishikawa.
source: Deming; Juran; Crosby; Ishikawa — quality management thinkers

front: Sources of power — the five bases (French & Raven)?

legend.meaning: Authority that comes from the role or title
Ability to give bonuses, recognition, or desirable assignments
Ability to punish or threaten negative consequences
Influence earned through knowledge, skill, and credibility
Influence from respect, charisma, or association with someone respected

back:
All five explain why people comply with a project manager who has little formal authority.

The key split: legitimate, reward and coercive power come WITH the position; expert and referent power are EARNED. Expert and referent are the most effective and durable; coercive is the weakest and a last resort.
tip: Earned beats granted: expert and referent are the strongest forms of power; coercive (penalty) is the weakest and a last resort.
source: French and Raven — the five bases of power

front: Stakeholder analysis — Power/Interest grid vs Salience vs Stakeholder Cube?

legend.meaning: Two dimensions, power x interest, giving four strategies: manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor
Three attributes — power, legitimacy, urgency — giving seven classes (definitive, dominant, dependent, and so on)
Three dimensions — power x interest x attitude (backer/blocker) — giving eight characters

back:
All three classify stakeholders so you can decide how to engage each one.

They differ in nuance: the grid uses two dimensions; salience adds legitimacy and urgency; the cube adds attitude. More dimensions capture more reality but take more effort — match the model to the complexity of your stakeholders.
tip: Power + interest = the grid; + legitimacy + urgency = salience; + attitude = the cube. See the Stakeholder Models guide for the diagrams.
source: Power/Interest grid; Mitchell-Agle-Wood salience; Murray-Webster and Simon stakeholder cube

front: Paths to mastery — Shu-Ha-Ri vs Dreyfus vs the Four Stages of Competence?

legend.meaning: Follow the rules, then break the rules, then transcend the rules — an agile adoption pattern
Five skill levels — novice, advanced beginner, competent, proficient, expert
Unconscious incompetence, conscious incompetence, conscious competence, unconscious competence

back:
All three describe the journey from beginner to mastery, where the highest level becomes intuitive and unconscious.

They differ in framing: Shu-Ha-Ri has three rules-centred stages from martial arts and agile; Dreyfus grades five levels of skill; the Four Stages of Competence is about your AWARENESS of your own (in)competence.
tip: All map novice to expert: Shu-Ha-Ri (three, rules-based), Dreyfus (five skill levels), Four Stages (awareness of competence).
source: Shu-Ha-Ri; Dreyfus; the Four Stages of Competence — learning and mastery models
